artifact.text:
This morning, on the omnibus going to the Sennaya, an elderly gentleman across from me — frock-coat, decent gloves, the sort of face one sees by the thousand in our city — was reading a newspaper, and at a certain paragraph his lips moved, and I saw him say to himself, almost inaudibly: *yes, yes, of course, we all know that.* Then he turned the page. He had been reading, I believe, about a famine somewhere; or about a regiment; or about a girl thrown from a window the night before in the Haymarket. It does not matter which. He had admitted it, and turned the page. I have not been able to put him out of my head all morning, and I see now I shall have to begin earlier in order to say why.

There was, I am told, a gathering of statesmen and writers somewhere abroad, where the question was put: can our order, our great and humane Western order, survive if it goes on pretending its conduct matches its profession? One gentleman said no — abolish the pretense, you will be left only with naked force, which is worse. Another, kindlier, said: let us at least admit the gap, candidly, in the right tone, before the right audience; this will be our renewal. And the panel applauded the second gentleman, and the matter was set down as half-resolved, and they went to dinner.

But I tell you — *vdrug*, with my old gentleman of the omnibus before me — there is a word the panel did not say, and it is the only word that touches the question. There is *admission*, which a lawyer prepares for his client; and there is *confession*, which Raskolnikov made on his knees in the Haymarket, kissing the earth he had befouled and saying aloud what he was. These are not two intensities of one act. They are different acts. Admission preserves the standing of the one who admits — that is its function, that is what it is *for*. Confession ends one self that another may, perhaps, begin. The Epilogue does not show the regeneration; the refusal is the whole point.

The order that admits, then, and admits, and admits — what does it produce? I tell you it produces precisely the cynicism into which a frank brute now steps and is felt, by half the world, as honesty. The brute is not the alternative to admission. The brute is admission's child. They are two faces of one refusal.

And here is the warning I owe you, because I once wrote it before and the censor would not let it through. I wrote a man named Stavrogin who composed the confession of his worst act, had it printed up, intended to circulate it. The bishop who read it refused him absolution — refused even to receive the document as confession — because the bishop saw at once: the form was humility, the substance was pride. The man was confessing in order to remain himself, only now decorated with the badge of one who had dared. It was *bezobrazie* in the strictest sense. It was the higher costume of свoевoлие.

So when our Western gentleman is urged to "acknowledge his hypocrisies" while keeping his weapons, his seat at the council, his capital, his civilizational vocabulary — when he is urged to add candor to his repertoire and call this his renewal — I say: this is Stavrogin's confession at scale. The one who has truly confessed cannot continue the act for which he confessed. This is the brutal grammatical fact, and every man in those rooms knows it, which is why no man in those rooms is in fact confessing.

I notice, too — I cannot help noticing — that in the same gathering one woman read out a list. *Looking away from genocide. Threatening to colonize an ally's territory. Kidnapping foreign heads of state.* These are not items in a column on the vulnerabilities of the order. These are children's tears. They are Ivan's ledger transcribed and read aloud at a conference, and I most respectfully return the ticket. The harmony whose preservation requires that such a list be tolerable as its footnote — what is that harmony? Whose?

But — and here I must press on the thing that holds me — there is no soul of the West that can kneel. There is no "I" of that magnitude. There are only particular faces with particular things on their hands: the foreign minister whose signature went on the paper, the editor who let the euphemism through, the academic who called the bombing a tragic event, my old gentleman on the omnibus this morning who turned the page. Confession is a порог. A single man, a single hour, a particular floor on which he kneels, and the certainty that the life he had before will not survive the act. The conferences cannot ask this, because the form of a conference is admission. The form of confession ends the conversation; it does not extend it across two more sessions in some pleasant southern city.

I do not say this with contempt. God forbid. I have stood under the white shroud and been given my life back at the trumpet, and I tell you — most do not receive such a gift as *gift*. Most receive it as a possession, and write essays afterward about the inconsistencies in the system that briefly threatened to take it from them. I know. I have been the man at the omnibus too. The page turns under one's own hand.

The hour, however, is open. Some particular man, somewhere, may yet kneel.

I leave the question at the threshold. I cannot finish it.
artifact.selected_form: Writer's Diary entry, opened on a remembered face.
artifact.stance: Implicated and unsparing; a man writing at his own table about what he himself has come close to refusing.
